factors.summary: Input costs are rising due to inflation and supply chain disruptions, creating cost pressures across multiple programs. The F-35 program is particularly impacted by inflation-related cost pressures, requiring supply chain improvements and affecting affordability. Additionally, supply chain performance delays are contributing to higher costs and lower production volumes on several programs, including the F-35 and F-16.

evidence.text: Heightened levels of inflation continue to present risk for Lockheed Martin and the broader industrial base. We have experienced impacts to our labor rates and suppliers have signaled inflation related cost pressures, which will flow through to our costs and pricing...
Current program challenges include ... inflation-related cost pressures, and the ability to continue to reduce the unit production costs and improve affordability.

Net sales decreased by approximately $945 million for the F-35 program due to lower volume on production contracts as a result of supply chain performance delays and delays in receiving additional contractual authorization and funding under the Lots 17 contract, and about $50 million on the F-16 program due to lower volume on sustainment contracts and an unfavorable profit adjustment on a production contract in the second quarter of 2022 as a result of manufacturing line ramp up delays, partially offset by higher volume on production contracts.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Rising input costs due to inflation and supply chain disruptions are causing cost pressures, particularly affecting major programs.

factors.summary: The margin trajectory is mixed across different segments. While some areas like Aeronautics and Missiles and Fire Control show improvements or stability due to favorable profit adjustments and contract mix, others such as the F-35 Program and inflationary pressures face challenges, leading to margin compression.

evidence.text: Aeronautics’ operating margin was 10.4% for the quarter ended June 26, 2022, up from 8.6% in the same period in 2021. Operating profit increased approximately $220 million on classified contracts due to a $225 million loss in the second quarter of 2021 on a classified program; and approximately $40 million for the F-22 program due to higher net favorable profit adjustments.

During the second quarter of 2022, we encountered supplier delays due to COVID-19 that negatively impacted F-35 net sales and operating profit in the second quarter due to lower volume and we expect the timing of F-35 net sales and operating profit will continue to be impacted in future periods.
evidence.subsection: ITEM 2. Unregistered Sales of Equity Securities and Use of Proceeds

evidence.text: MFC’s operating margin was positively impacted when compared to the second quarter of 2021 due to contract mix (lower SOF GLSS volume and lower development volume for tactical and strike missiles). Adjustments not related to volume, including net profit booking rate adjustments in the second quarter of 2022 were comparable to the same period in 2021.

factors.summary: The company is implementing cost reduction measures across various programs, with some success in improving operating profits, particularly in missiles and fire control. However, challenges such as supply chain issues, delays in contract definitization, and inflationary pressures are negatively impacting cost management efforts, leading to increased costs and delayed recovery of unrecognized sales.

evidence.text: MFC’s operating profit during the quarter ended June 26, 2022 increased $17 million, or 4%, compared to the same period in 2021. The increase was primarily attributable to higher operating profit of approximately $40 million for tactical and strike missile programs due to higher net favorable profit adjustments (Joint Air-to-Surface Standoff Missile (JASSM), High Mobility Artillery Rocket System (HIMARS), and Hellfire); and about $10 million for integrated air and missile defense programs due to higher net favorable profit adjustments (Patriot Advanced Capability-3 (PAC-3)).

These increases were partially offset by lower operating profit of approximately $145 million for the F-35 program due to lower volume on production contracts as a result of supply chain performance delays and delays in receiving additional contractual authorization and funding under the Lots 15-17 contract, and about $50 million on the F-16 program due to lower volume on sustainment contracts and an unfavorable profit adjustment on a production contract in the second quarter of 2022 as a result of manufacturing line ramp up delays, partially offset by higher volume on production contracts.
evidence.subsection: ITEM 2. Unregistered Sales of Equity Securities and Use of Proceeds

evidence.text: We are committed to our ongoing efforts to increase the efficiency of our operations and improve the cost competitiveness and affordability of our products and services...

We expect to recover the unrecognized sales and resume invoicing costs incurred upon receiving contractual authorization and funding on the production contract with the U.S. Government, which we expect to occur in the third quarter of 2022.

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: Cost reduction measures show some success but are hindered by supply chain issues and inflation.

factors.summary: The overall volume trends show a mixed performance across different programs. While certain programs, like the F-35, are expected to see gradual growth with a target of 156 deliveries by 2025, others are experiencing declines due to supply chain issues, contractual delays, and lower production volumes. The F-16 program and RMS programs have reported significant decreases in unit volumes, impacting net sales negatively.

evidence.text: Net sales decreased by approximately $945 million for the F-35 program due to lower volume on production contracts as a result of supply chain performance delays and delays in receiving additional contractual authorization and funding under the Lots 15-17 contract, and about $50 million on the F-16 program due to lower volume on sustainment contracts and an unfavorable profit adjustment on a production contract in the second quarter of 2022 as a result of manufacturing line ramp up delays, partially offset by higher volume on production contracts.

Product sales decreased $1.5 billion, or 10%, during the quarter ended June 26, 2022 compared to the same period in 2021. The decrease is primarily attributable to lower product sales of approximately $780 million at Aeronautics mostly due to lower volume on F-35 contracts...

RMS’ net sales during the six months ended June 26, 2022 decreased $785 million, or 9%, compared to the same period in 2021. The decrease was primarily attributable to lower net sales of approximately $310 million for TLS programs primarily due to the delivery of an international pilot training system in the first quarter of 2021 that did not recur in 2022; about $230 million for IWSS programs due to lower volume (LCS, TPQ-53 and Advanced Hawkeye); approximately $140 million for Sikorsky helicopter programs due to lower production volume (Black Hawk); and about $105 million for various C6ISR programs due to lower volume.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Volume trends show declines in several programs due to supply chain issues and contractual delays.

factors.summary: The supply chain health is facing significant challenges, primarily due to disruptions from COVID-19 and inflationary pressures, which have led to delays and reduced production volumes across multiple programs. These issues have negatively impacted net sales and operating profits, with particular mention of the F-35 and F-16 programs. Additionally, there are concerns about potential future disruptions affecting cash flows and contractual obligations.

evidence.text: Net sales decreased by approximately $945 million for the F-35 program due to lower volume on production contracts as a result of supply chain performance delays and delays in receiving additional contractual authorization and funding under the Lots 15-17 contract, and about $50 million on the F-16 program due to lower volume on sustainment contracts and an unfavorable profit adjustment on a production contract in the second quarter of 2022 as a result of manufacturing line ramp up delays, partially offset by higher volume on production contracts.

During the second quarter of 2022, we encountered supplier delays due to COVID-19 that negatively impacted F-35 net sales and operating profit in the second quarter due to lower volume and we expect the timing of F-35 net sales and operating profit will continue to be impacted in future periods.

During the first six months of 2022, our performance was adversely affected by supply chain disruptions and delays, as well as labor challenges associated with employee absences, travel restrictions, site access, quarantine restrictions, remote work, and adjusted work schedules...

The effects of COVID-19 have resulted in some negative impacts on our cash flows, partially due to supplier delays. The U.S. Government has taken certain actions and enacted legislation to mitigate the impacts of COVID-19 on public health, the economy, state and local governments, individuals, and businesses. Since the pandemic began, Lockheed Martin has remained committed to accelerating payments to the supply chain with a focus on small and at risk businesses. As of June 26, 2022, we have accelerated $1.0 billion of payments to our suppliers that are due by their terms in future periods.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Significant disruptions from COVID-19 and inflation are negatively impacting operations.

factors.summary: The company's free cash flow appears to be stable, as indicated by consistent generation trends. The focus is on returning cash to shareholders through share repurchases and investing in growth opportunities. There is evidence of positive free cash flow due to the share repurchase authorization.

evidence.text: The total remaining authorization for future common share repurchases under our share repurchase program was $1.6 billion as of June 26, 2022.
evidence.subsection: ITEM 2. Unregistered Sales of Equity Securities and Use of Proceeds

evidence.text: Free cash flow is a non-GAAP financial measure that we define as cash from operations less capital expenditures. Our capital expenditures are comprised of equipment and facilities infrastructure and information technology (inclusive of costs for the development or purchase of internal-use software that are capitalized). We use free cash flow to evaluate our business performance and overall liquidity, as well as a performance goal in our annual and long-term incentive plans. We believe free cash flow is a useful measure for investors because it represents the amount of cash generated from operations after reinvesting in the business and that may be available to return to stockholders and creditors (through dividends, stock repurchase and debt repayments) or available to fund acquisitions.
Other non-operating (expense) income, net primarily includes gains or losses related to changes in the fair value of strategic investments in companies made by our Lockheed Martin Ventures Fund.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: Consistent free cash flow generation supports stable cash deployment strategies.

Consistent with that strategy, we have continued to invest in our business and technologies through capital expenditures, independent research and development, and selective business acquisitions and investments. We have returned cash to stockholders through dividends and share repurchases.

evidence.text: We now expect FAS pension expense of approximately $1.1 billion in 2022, inclusive of the noncash, non-operating pension settlement charge of $1.5 billion (pretax) described above. Excluding the noncash, non-operating pension settlement charge, our expected FAS pension income will be approximately $410 million in 2022, which is $50 million lower than our prior 2022 FAS pension income estimate of $460 million.

The continued expectation is that final FY 2023 DoD funding will be higher than requested. Final legislation is not expected to be enacted until late in calendar year 2022 or, possibly, in early calendar year 2023.

We believe our cash and cash equivalents, our expected cash flow generated from operations and our access to credit markets will be sufficient to meet our cash requirements and cash deployment plans over the next twelve months and beyond based on our current business plans.

sentiment.rationale: Stable order flow with specific program delays impacting net sales.

factors.summary: Labor costs are mixed, with manageable costs due to a favorable contract agreement but also increasing due to inflation and absenteeism, leading to margin pressures.

evidence.text: We successfully negotiated a new four-year contract with the International Association of Machinists and Aerospace Workers covering approximately 5,000 represented employees that primarily work on F-35 production in Fort Worth, Texas in the second quarter of 2022.

We are actively engaging with our customers and are continuing to take measures to protect the health and safety of our employees. In our on-going effort to mitigate supply chain risks, we accelerated payments of $1.0 billion to our suppliers as of June 26, 2022...

We believe our cash and cash equivalents, our expected cash flow generated from operations and our access to credit markets will be sufficient to meet our cash requirements and cash deployment plans over the next twelve months and beyond based on our current business plans.

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: Manageable due to contract agreements but facing inflation and absenteeism pressures.

factors.summary: The company's organic growth is facing mixed challenges. While the F-35 program is experiencing delays and supplier issues, leading to constrained growth, other areas like net sales show stability without significant revenue changes. However, in the space segment, growth is decelerating due to lower sales volumes and reduced demand in key programs.

evidence.text: Deliveries are expected to remain in the range of 147-153 aircraft per year in 2023 and 2024, before we achieve our 156 aircraft delivery target in 2025. We continue to anticipate annual deliveries of 156 aircraft beyond 2025 for the foreseeable future.
Overall, our sales were negatively affected in the first six months of 2022 because of supply chain impacts.

Space’s net sales during the quarter ended June 26, 2022 decreased $352 million, or 11%, compared to the same period in 2021. The decrease was primarily attributable to lower net sales of approximately $425 million due to the previously announced renationalization of the AWE program on June 30, 2021, which was no longer included in our financial results beginning in the third quarter of 2021; and about $55 million for commercial civil space programs due to lower volume (Orion).

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Delays, supply chain issues, and decelerating growth in key areas.

factors.summary: The company's litigation exposure is not considered material, with no significant financial or operational impact reported. However, contingent obligations related to defined benefit plans and other legal proceedings are disclosed in the notes to the consolidated financial statements.

evidence.text: Litigation exposure is managed through defined benefit plan modifications and contingent obligations are disclosed in notes.

See 'Note 7 - Legal Proceedings and Contingencies' included in our Notes to Consolidated Financial Statements for information regarding our contingent obligations, including off-balance sheet arrangements.
evidence.subsection: Introduction / ITEM 2. Management’s Discussion and Analysis of Financial Condition and Results of Operations

evidence.text: The quality and safety of our products and services remain a top priority, and we have not experienced any significant incidents or recalls in the reported periods.
evidence.subsection: Introduction / ITEM 2. Management’s Discussion and Analysis of Financial Condition and Results of Operations

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: No significant impact reported, but contingent obligations exist.

factors.summary: The company has a significant debt load, which is being managed through refinancing and strategic pension liability management. Despite this, the debt leverage remains stable with no significant changes in borrowing levels reported, and the company remains in compliance with all covenants.

evidence.text: Our outstanding debt, net of unamortized discounts and issuance costs was $11.6 billion as of June 26, 2022 and is in the form of publicly-issued notes that bear interest at fixed rates. As of June 26, 2022, we were in compliance with all covenants contained in our debt and credit agreements.

We also continue to actively manage our debt levels, including maturities and interest rates, as evidenced by the debt transaction in the second quarter of 2022 and our pension obligations. We expect to continue to opportunistically manage our pension liabilities through the purchase of group annuity contracts for portions of our outstanding defined benefit pension obligations using assets from the pension trust as we did on June 24, 2022.

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: Stable debt levels with management through refinancing and pension obligations.

factors.summary: Market share appears to be stable, supported by international interest and program execution, though there are competitive pressures.

evidence.text: Germany announced its intention to purchase 35 F-35 aircraft. On March 28, 2022, the Government of Canada selected the F-35 as the preferred bidder to move into the Finalization Phase of the competitive process to replace their fighter fleet.
Substantially all of our contracts are accounted for using the percentage-of-completion cost-to-cost method.

We have a balanced cash deployment strategy to invest in our business and key technologies to provide our customers with enhanced capabilities, enhance stockholder value, and position ourselves to take advantage of new business opportunities when they arise. Consistent with that strategy, we have continued to invest in our business and technologies through capital expenditures, independent research and development, and selective business acquisitions and investments.

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: Stable market share with international interest but facing competitive pressures.

factors.summary: Geopolitical factors are presenting both opportunities and risks, particularly in the context of international sales and the impact of the conflict in Ukraine. While increased interest in products and services due to heightened security concerns is noted, there are also risks associated with geopolitical tensions and sanctions.

evidence.text: Germany announced its intention to purchase 35 F-35 aircraft. On March 28, 2022, the Government of Canada selected the F-35 as the preferred bidder to move into the Finalization Phase of the competitive process to replace their fighter fleet.

Russia’s invasion of Ukraine has elevated global geopolitical tensions and security concerns. As a result, we have received increased interest for our products and services as countries seek to improve their security posture, particularly in Europe...

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: Geopolitical factors present opportunities due to increased security interest, outweighing associated risks.

factors.summary: The revenue mix is shifting towards higher-margin segments, particularly defense and space systems, as well as tactical and strike missiles, which are contributing to margin improvements.

evidence.text: Our main areas of focus are in defense, space, intelligence, homeland security and information technology, including cybersecurity.

MFC’s operating profit during the quarter ended June 26, 2022 increased $17 million, or 4%, compared to the same period in 2021. The increase was primarily attributable to higher operating profit of approximately $40 million for tactical and strike missile programs due to higher net favorable profit adjustments (Joint Air-to-Surface Standoff Missile (JASSM), High Mobility Artillery Rocket System (HIMARS), and Hellfire); and about $10 million for integrated air and missile defense programs due to higher net favorable profit adjustments (Patriot Advanced Capability-3 (PAC-3)).

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: Shift towards higher-margin segments contributes to margin improvements.

factors.summary: The company demonstrates strong pricing power through the use of contractual terms and economic price adjustment clauses, which help maintain profitability even when sales volumes decrease. Higher operating profits in certain missile programs further indicate effective cost management and pricing strategies.

evidence.text: For new contract proposals, we are factoring into our pricing heightened levels of inflation based on accepted DoD escalation indices and other assumptions, and in some cases seeking the inclusion of economic price adjustment (EPA) clauses, which would permit, subject to the particular contractual terms, cost adjustments in fixed price contracts for unexpected inflation.

MFC’s operating profit during the quarter ended June 26, 2022 increased $17 million, or 4%, compared to the same period in 2021. The increase was primarily attributable to higher operating profit of approximately $40 million for tactical and strike missile programs due to higher net favorable profit adjustments (Joint Air-to-Surface Standoff Missile (JASSM), High Mobility Artillery Rocket System (HIMARS), and Hellfire); and about $10 million for integrated air and missile defense programs due to higher net favorable profit adjustments (Patriot Advanced Capability-3 (PAC-3)).

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: Strong pricing power through contractual terms and cost management leads to higher profits.

factors.summary: Capacity utilization is being managed through adjustments in production volumes to align with contract requirements and market demand. Despite some decreases in net sales across various programs, overall stability is maintained.

evidence.text: Our programs continued to be well supported and funded through the FY 2022 budget process.

RMS’ net sales during the quarter ended June 26, 2022 decreased $230 million, or 5%, compared to the same period in 2021. The decrease was primarily attributable to lower net sales of approximately $100 million for Sikorsky helicopter programs due to lower production volume (Black Hawk); about $80 million for integrated warfare systems and sensors (IWSS) programs due to lower volume (Littoral Combat Ship (LCS) and Advanced Hawkeye); and approximately $55 million for various C6ISR programs due to lower volume.

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: Stable management with adjustments, but some decreases in net sales.

factors.summary: The end market momentum for defense and space is mixed. While there is growth driven by geopolitical tensions and increased government funding, particularly in strategic and missile defense programs, there are also declines in other areas such as national security space programs and lower equity earnings from investments like United Launch Alliance (ULA).

evidence.text: The U.S. Government and other nations have implemented broad economic sanctions and export controls targeting Russia, which, combined with the conflict, have the potential to indirectly disrupt our supply chain and access to certain resources...

Space’s operating profit during the quarter ended June 26, 2022 decreased $67 million, or 20%, compared to the same period in 2021. The decrease was primarily attributable to approximately $55 million for national security space programs primarily due to lower net favorable profit adjustments (primarily Space-Based Infrared System (SBIRS) and classified programs); and about $40 million of lower equity earnings from our investment in United Launch Alliance (ULA).

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: Mixed growth and declines across different market areas.

factors.summary: Product quality and safety are maintained, with no significant incidents or recalls reported.

evidence.text: The quality and safety of our products and services remain a top priority, and we have not experienced any significant incidents or recalls in the reported periods.

evidence.text: We believe our cash and cash equivalents, our expected cash flow generated from operations and our access to credit markets will be sufficient to meet our cash requirements and cash deployment plans over the next twelve months and beyond based on our current business plans.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: No significant incidents, indicating strong product quality and safety.

factors.summary: The company is actively managing inventory levels to mitigate supply chain risks, particularly by accelerating payments to suppliers. However, there is no significant mention of excess or shortage risks in the liquidity and cash flows section.

evidence.text: In our on-going effort to mitigate supply chain risks, we accelerated payments of $1.0 billion to our suppliers as of June 26, 2022, that are due according to contractual terms in future periods, while consistently prioritizing small businesses...

Our principal source of liquidity is our cash from operations. However, we also have access to credit markets, if needed, for liquidity or general corporate purposes, including our revolving credit facility or the ability to issue commercial paper, and letters of credit to support customer advance payments and for other trade finance purposes such as guaranteeing our performance on particular contracts.

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: Active management with no significant excess or shortage risks.

factors.summary: Competitive dynamics are stable and managed effectively through technological differentiation and strategic investments, which help maintain a competitive edge. The company focuses on key areas such as defense, space, intelligence, homeland security, and information technology, including cybersecurity.

evidence.text: Our main areas of focus are in defense, space, intelligence, homeland security and information technology, including cybersecurity.

We have a balanced cash deployment strategy to invest in our business and key technologies to provide our customers with enhanced capabilities, enhance stockholder value, and position ourselves to take advantage of new business opportunities when they arise. Consistent with that strategy, we have continued to invest in our business and technologies through capital expenditures, independent research and development, and selective business acquisitions and investments.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: Effective management through technological differentiation and strategic investments.

factors.summary: The company demonstrates strong differentiation through its technological leadership, scale, and customer relationships, which are highlighted as key competitive advantages. The evidence underscores the importance of maintaining quality and safety, as well as strategically investing in technology and business opportunities to enhance capabilities and stockholder value.

evidence.text: The quality and safety of our products and services remain a top priority, and we have not experienced any significant incidents or recalls in the reported periods.

evidence.text: We have a balanced cash deployment strategy to invest in our business and key technologies to provide our customers with enhanced capabilities, enhance stockholder value, and position ourselves to take advantage of new business opportunities when they arise. Consistent with that strategy, we have continued to invest in our business and technologies through capital expenditures, independent research and development, and selective business acquisitions and investments.

factors.summary: The company exhibits high customer concentration, primarily relying on the U.S. Government, especially the Department of Defense, for a significant portion of its revenue. However, the liquidity and cash flows section suggests a strategy aimed at diversification through investments in technologies and geographies, indicating an effort to reduce reliance on a single customer base.

evidence.text: During the six months ended June 26, 2022, 73% of our $30.4 billion in net sales were from the U.S. Government, either as a prime contractor or as a subcontractor (including 64% from the Department of Defense (DoD))...

We have a balanced cash deployment strategy to invest in our business and key technologies to provide our customers with enhanced capabilities, enhance stockholder value, and position ourselves to take advantage of new business opportunities when they arise. Consistent with that strategy, we have continued to invest in our business and technologies through capital expenditures, independent research and development, and selective business acquisitions and investments.
